evidence: Model classes are precisely named: DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, CollocateProfileComparator, ExplorationResult, ComparisonResult all communicate domain intent without ambiguity.

QueryExecutor interface method names are well-differentiated: findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery each signal a distinct behaviour rather than sharing a generic verb.

handleRelationQueryInternal (SketchHandlers.java:180) uses 'Internal' as a suffix that communicates access level, not behaviour — the method handles a single-relation sketch query for a given RelationType; a name like handleSingleRelationQuery would say what it does.
scoreNouns (SemanticFieldExplorer.java:241) is vague: it ranks discovered nouns by how many seed collocates they share. A name such as rankNounsBySharedCollocates would carry the criterion.

buildNounProfiles (SemanticFieldExplorer.java:219) has an inverted name: it returns Map<collocate, Map<noun, logDice>>, i.e. an index of collocates to per-noun scores, not a profile per noun. buildCollocateToNounIndex or groupNounsByCollocate would be accurate.

f_xy and f_y (BlackLabQueryExecutor.java:266-277) use mathematical formula notation instead of Java naming conventions. The rest of the codebase consistently uses descriptive camelCase. These variables are confined to a few lines in scoreHits and are documented by the logDice formula context, but they are still an outlier.
LongIntHashMap, CqlUtils, PatternSubstitution, HttpApiUtils, ConlluConverter, GrammarConfigLoader are all file/class names that precisely describe their content.

The handle* prefix on HTTP handler methods (handleSemanticFieldExplore, handleSketchRequest, handleConcordanceExamples, etc.) forms a consistent, recognisable convention across SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers.

strengths: Domain model classes (DiscoveredNoun, CoreCollocate, AdjectiveProfile, ExplorationResult) are precisely named and communicate intent at a glance.
QueryExecutor interface methods are well-differentiated — findCollocations, executeSurfacePattern, executeDependencyPattern, executeBcqlQuery each name a distinct operation without overlap.
HTTP handler methods adopt a consistent handle* prefix convention across SketchHandlers and ExplorationHandlers, making routing structure immediately readable.
Utility and infrastructure classes (LongIntHashMap, CqlUtils, PatternSubstitution, GrammarConfigLoader, ConlluConverter) have precise, self-describing class names.
naming_quality.issue_character: The naming issues are isolated to a small number of private helper methods in the exploration subsystem and a localised math-notation variable cluster in the query executor; they reflect minor lapses in naming discipline on implementation details rather than a systemic pattern affecting the public API surface.
naming_quality.score_rationale: The codebase sits well above average: the public API surface, domain model, and infrastructure layers are named with care and consistency, which is where naming quality matters most to maintainers. What pulls the score down from the 80-85 range is a cluster of private method names that either use weak suffixes (Internal), vague verbs (scoreNouns), or invert the abstraction they describe (buildNounProfiles), plus mathematical variable notation (f_xy, f_y) that diverges from the camelCase convention used everywhere else. These are real issues that cost a developer cognitive effort when navigating the exploration module, but they are confined to a single subsystem and are all fixable with targeted renames.

issues.summary: handleRelationQueryInternal uses 'Internal' suffix to signal access scope rather than behaviour
evidence: private void handleRelationQueryInternal(HttpExchange exchange, String lemma, String relationId, RelationType relationType) at line 180 is called by both handleRelationSketch and handleDependencyRelationQuery as a shared dispatch point.
The suffix 'Internal' communicates nothing about what the method does; it merely signals that it is not part of the public interface, which is already conveyed by the private modifier.
issues.suggestion: Rename to handleSingleRelationQuery or querySingleRelationAndRespond to describe the action rather than the access level.

issues.summary: scoreNouns does not name the scoring criterion and reads as generic as 'process'
evidence: private List<DiscoveredNoun> scoreNouns(Map<String, Map<String, Double>> nounProfiles, int minShared) at line 241 ranks discovered nouns by how many seed collocates they share.
The verb 'score' is nearly as uninformative as 'process' in this context; the criterion (shared collocate count) is the meaningful part of the name.
issues.suggestion: Rename to rankNounsBySharedCollocates or discoverNounsByCollocateOverlap so the criterion is visible in the call site.

issues.summary: buildNounProfiles returns a collocate-keyed index, not a per-noun profile
evidence: private Map<String, Map<String, Double>> buildNounProfiles(...) at line 219 returns Map<collocateLemma, Map<noun, logDice>>.
The return type is indexed by collocate (outer key) and noun (inner key), so a name like buildCollocateToNounIndex or groupNounsByCollocate would accurately describe the structure.
issues.suggestion: Rename to buildCollocateToNounIndex (or groupNounsByCollocate) to match the actual shape of the returned data structure.

issues.summary: f_xy and f_y use formula-style names inconsistent with the codebase's camelCase convention
evidence: long f_xy = 0L; and long f_y = ... at lines 266-273 in scoreHits, and long f_xy = entry.getValue(); at line 467 in buildAndRankCollocates.

Every other variable in the file uses standard camelCase (collocateLemma, headwordFreq, freqMap, logDice). The underscore-separated math names are an outlier that requires the reader to mentally switch notation.
issues.suggestion: Rename f_xy to cooccurrenceFreq (or pairFreq) and f_y to collocateFreq to align with the codebase naming convention while still being descriptive.

root_causes: Private implementation helpers received less naming attention than public API methods; the exploration module in particular grew through iterative refactoring and some names were not updated to reflect their final responsibility.
The logDice scoring code imported mathematical notation from the formula definition without translating it into the project's naming idiom.
